questions.question: Explain the difference between Monte Carlo and Las Vegas randomized algorithms. Give one example of each.
keyPoints: Monte Carlo: May produce incorrect result but runs within guaranteed time
Las Vegas: Always produces correct result but running time is random
Example Monte Carlo: Randomized quicksort (may sort incorrectly but usually fast)
Example Las Vegas: Randomized quicksort (always sorts correctly, runtime varies)

questions.question: State and explain the time complexity of the Heapsort algorithm. Why is it considered an in-place sorting algorithm?
keyPoints: Time complexity: O(n log n) in all cases (best, average, worst)
Extract max operation takes O(log n) and is performed n times
In-place: Only uses O(1) extra space, sorts within the input array

questions.question: Trace through the Heapsort algorithm step by step to sort the array [12, 7, 18, 5, 15] in ascending order. Show the initial heap construction, each heapify operation, and the final sorted array.
keyPoints: Build max-heap from unsorted array using bottom-up heapify
Show heap structure after building phase: [18, 15, 12, 5, 7]
Extract maximum element and restore heap property for each iteration

questions.question: Explain the Randomized Quicksort algorithm. Analyze its expected time complexity using probabilistic analysis, assuming all input permutations are equally likely. How does it compare to the deterministic Quicksort in terms of performance?
keyPoints: Randomized Quicksort randomly selects pivot element instead of fixed position
Expected time complexity: O(n log n) - derived from expected number of comparisons
Uses indicator random variables and linearity of expectation for analysis
Advantage: Avoids worst-case O(n²) by randomizing input, provides good average case performance
